explanations.LEXICAL AMBIGUITY: The word 'आम' means both 'mango' (fruit) and 'common/ordinary'. Without context, both readings are valid.

possible_meanings: He eats mango.
He does common/ordinary things.
most_likely: He eats mango — the fruit-eating interpretation is more natural in everyday speech.

sentence: मैंने उसे देखा जब वो बाजार में था

explanations.REFERENTIAL AMBIGUITY: The pronoun 'उसे' and 'वो' are both third-person references, but it is unclear who 'he' refers to — the person seen or someone else in the market.

possible_meanings: I saw him when he was in the market (same person).
I saw him (person A) when he (person B) was in the market.
most_likely: I saw him when he (same person) was in the market.

sentence: राम और श्याम की बहन आई

explanations.SYNTACTIC AMBIGUITY: The possessive 'की' is ambiguous — it can attach to 'श्याम' only or to 'राम और श्याम' together. This is a coordination ambiguity.

possible_meanings: Ram's sister and Shyam's sister both came (two sisters).
The sister of both Ram and Shyam came (one shared sister).
most_likely: Ambiguous — both readings are equally valid without context.

explanations.SCOPE AMBIGUITY: 'कल' (tomorrow) and 'नहीं' (negation) have unclear scope. It is ambiguous whether the negation applies to coming tomorrow specifically or to coming at all.

possible_meanings: Tomorrow I will not come (but I may come on another day).
I will not come tomorrow (strong refusal with emphasis on tomorrow).
most_likely: Tomorrow I will not come — the most common conversational interpretation.

sentence: Usne apni car mein unhe choda

explanations.PRAGMATIC AMBIGUITY: The verb 'choda' in Hinglish is phonologically ambiguous — it can mean 'dropped off/gave a ride' (छोड़ा) or carry a vulgar connotation (छोदा) depending on speaker intent and context.

possible_meanings: He dropped them off in his car (gave a ride).
Vulgar interpretation due to phonological similarity.
most_likely: He dropped them off in his car — the neutral transportation meaning is most likely in polite conversation.

explanations.REFERENTIAL AMBIGUITY: 'वो' in the subordinate clause can refer to either the subject 'उसने' (he himself) or a different third person.

possible_meanings: He said that he (himself) will go.
He said that someone else will go.
most_likely: He said that he himself will go — reflexive interpretation is most common.

sentence: मुझे पता नहीं था कि वो क्या करेगा

explanations.SCOPE AMBIGUITY: The negation 'नहीं' can scope over 'पता' (didn't know) or over the entire embedded clause (knew but not what).

possible_meanings: I had no idea what he would do (total ignorance).
I knew something but not specifically what he would do.
most_likely: I had no idea what he would do.

explanations.LEXICAL AMBIGUITY: 'हल्का' can mean 'lightweight' (physical weight), 'light-colored', or 'trivial/not serious' depending on context.

possible_meanings: It/he is very lightweight (physical).
It is very light-colored.
It is very trivial/not serious.
most_likely: Context-dependent — all three meanings are equally valid.

explanations.MORPHOLOGICAL AMBIGUITY: 'कल' as a morpheme is temporally symmetric — it means both 'yesterday' and 'tomorrow' in Hindi. Only the tense of 'आया था' (past) resolves it to 'yesterday' here.

possible_meanings: I had come yesterday.
Without tense context, 'कल' alone is ambiguous between yesterday and tomorrow.
most_likely: I had come yesterday — resolved by the past tense verb.

explanations.PRAGMATIC AMBIGUITY: This question can be a genuine inquiry about residence or an indirect speech act implying 'you should help me since you live nearby' or even sarcasm.

possible_meanings: A genuine question: Do you live here?
Indirect implication: Since you live here, you should know/help.
Sarcastic: You act as if you own the place.
most_likely: A genuine question about residence in most neutral contexts.

explanations.SCOPE AMBIGUITY: Quantifier 'सब' (all) and negation 'नहीं' have ambiguous scope: not all came, OR all did not come (none came).

possible_meanings: Not all people came (some came, some didn't).
All people did not come (nobody came).
most_likely: Not all people came — partial negation is the more natural reading.

explanations.SEMANTIC AMBIGUITY: The phrase is semantically ambiguous between literal meaning (eyes literally speak) and metaphorical meaning (eyes are expressive). The semantic interpretation depends on figurative vs literal reading.

possible_meanings: Literal (impossible): Her eyes literally speak/talk.
Metaphorical: Her eyes are very expressive.
most_likely: Metaphorical — her eyes are very expressive.

explanations.SEMANTIC AMBIGUITY: 'दिल टूटना' is semantically ambiguous between literal (heart broke physically) and idiomatic (heartbreak/emotional pain). The semantic meaning depends on figurative interpretation.

possible_meanings: Literal: My heart physically broke.
Idiomatic: I am heartbroken / emotionally devastated.
most_likely: Idiomatic — I am heartbroken.

possible_meanings: He/she added salt.
most_likely: He/she added salt — clear, unambiguous sentence.

possible_meanings: Ram went home.
most_likely: Ram went home — clear, unambiguous sentence.

explanations.PRAGMATIC AMBIGUITY: The imperative 'पानी लाओ' can be a command, a polite request, or an urgent plea depending entirely on the speaker's tone and context.

possible_meanings: Command: Bring water (now).
Polite request: Please bring some water.
Urgent plea: Bring water quickly (emergency).
most_likely: A direct request to bring water — exact pragmatic force depends on tone.

explanations.PRAGMATIC AMBIGUITY: This can be a genuine question, a rhetorical doubt, or an indirect expression of hope or worry about someone coming.

possible_meanings: Genuine question: Will he come?
Rhetorical doubt: I wonder if he will come (skepticism).
Hopeful: Do you think he will come? (indirect hope)
most_likely: A genuine question asking whether he will come.

sentence: उस पर्वत पर बादल थे जो बहुत ऊँचा था

explanations.SYNTACTIC AMBIGUITY: The relative clause 'जो बहुत ऊँचा था' can attach to 'पर्वत' (the mountain was very high) or to 'बादल' (the clouds were very high). This is a classic PP/relative clause attachment ambiguity.

possible_meanings: On that very high mountain, there were clouds.
On that mountain, there were very high clouds.
most_likely: On that very high mountain, there were clouds — attachment to mountain is more natural.

explanations.REFERENTIAL AMBIGUITY: 'उसने' and 'उसे' are both third-person pronouns with no clear antecedent established. Who hit whom is entirely unclear without prior context.

possible_meanings: He/she (A) hit him/her (B).
He/she (B) hit him/her (A) — depending on who 'usne' refers to.
most_likely: Cannot be resolved without prior discourse context establishing referents.

explanations.PHONOLOGICAL AMBIGUITY: In this Hinglish/transliterated form, 'kal' appears twice with the same phonological form but means different things — 'yesterday's work' and 'do it tomorrow'. The word 'कल' is a phonological homonym meaning both yesterday and tomorrow.

possible_meanings: Do yesterday's work tomorrow (procrastination advice).
Do tomorrow's work tomorrow (do not rush).
most_likely: Intentionally paradoxical — 'Do yesterday's work tomorrow' as a humorous or philosophical statement.

sentence: वो लड़की जो वहाँ खड़ी है मेरी बहन है
possible_meanings: That girl who is standing there is my sister.
most_likely: That girl who is standing there is my sister — unambiguous.

sentence: मेरी माँ और बाप की तस्वीर

explanations.SYNTACTIC AMBIGUITY: Coordination ambiguity: 'की तस्वीर' can scope over just 'बाप' (a picture of father, and separately mother) or over 'माँ और बाप' together (a picture of both mother and father together).

possible_meanings: A picture of my mother, and a picture of my father (two separate pictures).
A picture of my mother and father together (one picture).
most_likely: A picture of my mother and father together — joint possession reading is most natural.

explanations.MORPHOLOGICAL AMBIGUITY: 'खेला' can be morphologically analyzed as: (1) verb past tense 'played', or (2) nominal form 'a game/play'. The word form is identical but morphological category differs.

possible_meanings: The child played (verb: past tense).
The child's game/play (nominal use).
most_likely: The child played — verbal past tense reading is most common.

possible_meanings: The weather is very good today.
most_likely: The weather is very good today — unambiguous.

possible_meanings: He/she ate the apple.
most_likely: He/she ate the apple — unambiguous.
